metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The plan says to add `schema=model_cls` at `structured.py:161`, but that line lives inside `tolerant_parse(text: str)`, which has NO `model_cls` parameter. The variable `model_cls` does not exist in that function's scope. Implementing the schema-guided repair requires either (a) adding `model_cls` as a parameter to `tolerant_parse` and threading it through every call site, or (b) adding it to `parse_structured` and threading there. Neither API change is described, making the delta structurally incomplete as written.
findings.location: Output self-heal section — 'structured.py:161 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True) WITHOUT schema= — add schema=model_cls'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the schema-guided repair_json call into `parse_structured` (after `tolerant_parse` returns but before `validate_to`), or add `model_cls=None` to `tolerant_parse` and pass it from `parse_structured`; document the chosen API change explicitly.

findings.finding: `tolerant_parse(text: str)` at structured.py has no `model_cls` parameter — the `repair_json` call at line 161 is inside this function and has no `model_cls` in scope. The plan proposes adding `schema=model_cls` at that call site without describing the required signature change to `tolerant_parse`, making the integration non-implementable as written.
findings.location: Output self-heal section (What): 'structured.py:161 currently calls repair_json(cand, return_objects=True) WITHOUT a schema= arg — add schema=model_cls'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the schema-guided repair into `parse_structured`: call `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=..., schema_repair_mode='standard')` inside `parse_structured` after `tolerant_parse` raises, or add `model_cls: type | None = None` to `tolerant_parse` and pass it through from `parse_structured`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_structured.py` to the Files edit-target list (or explicitly state `model_cls` will be an optional keyword arg with `None` default so existing single-arg call sites are unaffected).
findings.finding: The plan threads model_cls into tolerant_parse but does not enumerate tests/unit/test_structured.py as a required update: that file calls structured.tolerant_parse(text) directly at 7+ call sites (lines 43, 50, 56, 61, 139, 157, 258, 283) with only the text argument. If model_cls is added as a required positional parameter, all those calls break; if optional, the plan should say so. Either way, test_structured.py is an unenumerated consumer outside the named edit-target files.
findings.location: Files section / Self-heal tweak 1 — 'requires threading model_cls into the tolerant-parse helper'
findings.suggested_fix: Make the parameter optional: `def tolerant_parse(text: str, model_cls=None) -> Any:` and use it only inside the repair_json call when non-None. This preserves backward compatibility for all existing callers and requires no edits to test_structured.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `tolerant_parse` gains a `model_cls` parameter (and list callsites to update) or whether schema-guided repair is applied in a new helper above `tolerant_parse`, so the implementation decision is not left to the executor.
findings.finding: `tolerant_parse` currently has the signature `tolerant_parse(text: str)` and is called from `parse_structured(text, model_cls)`. The plan says 'requires threading `model_cls` into the tolerant-parse helper' but never specifies the resulting signature change or whether the schema-guided repair call lives inside `tolerant_parse` (requiring a signature break and callsite updates) vs. inline in the repair loop in `_pai_structured` before calling the existing `tolerant_parse`.
findings.location: What section — 'Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair'; Files section: `src/rebar/llm/structured.py`

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a log.warning (or increment a counter/metric) in the except branch of the schema-guided repair fallback so operators can detect when json-repair's schema feature is failing silently.
findings.finding: The schema-guided json-repair fallback swallows the exception silently: when the `schema=model_cls` call raises, the plan's try/except falls back to schema-less repair with no log, metric, or trace — operators cannot tell whether the schema-guided feature is working or silently broken in production.
findings.location: Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair / structured.py:161 / AC: test_repair_json_falls_back_when_schema_call_raises
findings.suggested_fix: In the except handler: `logger.warning('schema-guided repair_json raised %s; falling back to schema-less repair', exc); metrics.increment('json_repair.schema_fallback')` — then the existing schema-less call. The unit test for the fallback AC should assert the warning is emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended exception type(s) for the schema-guided `repair_json` fallback (e.g. `except Exception` with a log-and-fallback comment, or a narrower set such as `except (TypeError, ValueError)`) so the executor has a concrete default to implement.
findings.finding: The `try/except` wrapping the schema-guided `repair_json` call does not specify which exception type(s) to catch. A bare `except Exception` silently swallows unexpected failures (e.g. import errors, logic bugs in json-repair), while a too-narrow catch (e.g. `TypeError` only) would miss schema-validation errors from the library. The plan says 'falls back to today's schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises' without naming the exception scope.
findings.location: What > Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the tweak-1 bullet: 'catch `except Exception as exc` (log at DEBUG level, then call schema-less `repair_json`) so any library-internal error degrades gracefully without masking bugs in the outer call stack'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the prior-art framing for self-heal tweak 2: cite a source that specifically includes the raw faulty output in the reask (not just the error), or downgrade 'proven' to 'plausible / adopted by [source]'; the Instructor citation only supports error-only reask.
findings.finding: The plan invokes 'the LangChain RetryWithErrorOutputParser / Instructor pattern' as prior-art justification for including the raw faulty model output in the reask prompt, but the cited prior art (Instructor's reask) sends only the validation error, not the raw prior output. The structured-output research doc (§2) confirms: Instructor appends `'errors encountered:\n{e}'` — the error string only. Including the raw output is an extension beyond the cited pattern, and the plan labels it 'proven' without evidence that the incremental output-inclusion improves reask success rates.
findings.location: What section, 'Self-heal tweak 2 — faulty prior output in the reask'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale comment in the code (or plan) for _FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS=2000 (e.g., derived from LangChain/Instructor defaults, or a simple token-budget estimate), so future maintainers have a reference when tuning.
findings.finding: The _FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS = 2000 truncation constant is asserted without any measurement rationale — no data on what snippet length actually helps the model self-correct vs. just bloating the reask prompt.
findings.location: Self-heal tweak 2 — faulty prior output in the reask / runner.py:530 / AC: test_reask_prompt_includes_bounded_prior_output
findings.suggested_fix: Add a code comment: `# 2000 chars ≈ ~500 tokens; keeps reask under model context limits while covering most validation-error context (cf. LangChain RetryWithErrorOutputParser). Tune if reask token budgets change.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which `schema_repair_mode` value to pass and briefly describe what it controls (e.g. 'standard' = coerce-and-fill vs 'strict' = raise-on-violation), so the AC and the test can pin it explicitly.
findings.finding: The `schema_repair_mode` processing parameter is referenced alongside `schema=model_cls` in the What section but its value vocabulary is never pinned as a decision rule: the body uses `'standard'` once (parenthetically noting it is the default), but the ACs, the structured.py change description, and the experiment log never specify which value must be passed nor what behavioral difference a non-default value would produce.
findings.location: What section — 'Self-heal tweak 1'; Verified library behavior section listing `schema_repair_mode` parameter
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC #2, add: 'Pass `schema_repair_mode=\'standard\'` (the default; coerce-and-fill behavior) — no need to pass it explicitly unless a non-default mode is intended; if so, name the mode and add a test asserting the call signature.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies `pyproject.toml` declares `json-repair>=0.61`, e.g. a static check or a note that `test_repair_json_receives_schema` asserts the `schema=` kwarg is accepted without error.
findings.finding: The pin bump from `json-repair>=0.30` to `>=0.61` is a described deliverable (named in 'What' and 'Files') but has no direct acceptance criterion verifying it was applied — its correctness is only implied by `test_repair_json_receives_schema` passing.
findings.location: What > Self-heal tweak 1; Files: pyproject.toml; Acceptance Criteria (all bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: '`pyproject.toml` declares `json-repair>=0.61` (verified by `test_repair_json_receives_schema` importing and calling `repair_json(…, schema=model_cls)` without a TypeError).' This ties the pin to an observable test failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the NativeOutput stop-reason parity change, e.g. a feature flag, a note on the revert commit strategy, or confirmation that no production callers currently consume NativeOutput hollow returns (with evidence).
findings.finding: The NativeOutput behavioral change — converting previously silent hollow returns into raised `UnretryableOutputError` exceptions — is an explicitly acknowledged breaking change for any caller on the NativeOutput path, but the plan provides no rollback/back-out mechanism beyond framing it as a correctness fix.
findings.location: What > NativeOutput stop-reason parity (the core fix); Acceptance Criteria bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Document in the plan: 'NativeOutput callers are limited to [list/path]; all are covered by the test suite; back-out is `git revert <sha>` with no data migration needed.' Alternatively, gate the check behind a flag for one release cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a behavioral smoke test (not just a parameter-name check) confirming that `repair_json('{"x": 1}', schema=<PydanticModelClass>)` returns a correctly-typed result with json-repair >=0.61, and document the accepted type (model class vs. model_json_schema dict) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'json-repair 0.61.2 accepts a pydantic v2 model directly' via schema=model_cls, but the experiment log only confirms the parameter NAME 'schema' is present in the function signature — it does not confirm that passing a Pydantic v2 model class object (as opposed to a JSON-schema dict or other type) is the supported calling convention. No lockfile, package source, or executable artifact in the repo can ground this behavioral claim.
findings.location: Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner offline experiment: `from json_repair import repair_json; from pydantic import BaseModel; class M(BaseModel): x: int; print(repair_json('{x: 1}', return_objects=True, schema=M))` and include the captured stdout in the experiment log. If json-repair expects a JSON Schema dict, change the plan to pass `model_cls.model_json_schema()` instead of `model_cls`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact type that json-repair 0.61.2's `schema=` parameter accepts (Pydantic model class vs. JSON Schema dict from `model_cls.model_json_schema()`), and update the plan to pass the correct type — the experiment log confirms only the parameter name, not the accepted argument type.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json(cand, schema=model_cls)` passes the Pydantic model class object directly, but json-repair 0.61.2's `schema=` parameter conventionally accepts a JSON Schema dict (e.g. `model_cls.model_json_schema()`), not a raw Pydantic class. If the library expects a dict, passing a class will either raise every time (caught by try/except -> silently always falls back to schema-less repair, meaning the schema-guided feature never operates) or be silently ignored. The experiment log only confirms the parameter NAME `schema` exists — it does not verify that a Pydantic model class is the accepted type.
findings.location: What > Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair; structured.py:161
findings.suggested_fix: Change `schema=model_cls` to `schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()` if json-repair's `schema=` expects a JSON Schema dict (the conventional interface), and add a one-line verification note to the experiment log confirming the accepted type (e.g. `repair_json.__doc__` or a minimal call with a known schema dict).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `schema=model_cls` capability assertion with a concrete artifact: a pip-freeze / installed-package output showing json-repair 0.61.2, plus a minimal live call `repair_json('{"x": 1}', schema=MyPydanticModel)` that verifies the parameter accepts a Pydantic BaseModel class (not a JSON Schema dict) and produces schema-guided output — or adjust the implementation to pass `model_cls.model_json_schema()` if json-repair expects a dict.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'json-repair 0.61.2 accepts a pydantic v2 model directly' for the `schema=model_cls` argument, but the experiment log only verifies the PARAMETER NAME 'schema' is present in the function signature — not that json-repair actually accepts a Pydantic BaseModel class (versus, e.g., a JSON Schema dict, which is the conventional input for JSON validation tooling). If json-repair's `schema=` expects a dict, passing a model class will either silently misfire or raise at runtime; the try/except fallback would swallow the error, masking the bug.
findings.location: What section, 'Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Check json-repair's source or docs for what `schema=` actually accepts. If it expects a JSON Schema dict, change the plan to pass `schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()` instead of `schema=model_cls`. Add a minimal live-call artifact (captured output, not just a signature inspection) to the 'Verified library behavior' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm empirically (e.g. `python -c "from json_repair import repair_json; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(repair_json))"` and then a short live call with `schema=SomePydanticModel`) that json-repair 0.61.2's `schema=` parameter accepts a Pydantic v2 BaseModel class directly, OR switch the call to `schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()` if it expects a JSON Schema dict — and add a test assertion that verifies the schema-guided path actually produces a schema-conformant output (not just that the kwarg was passed).
findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json(cand, schema=model_cls)` passes a Pydantic v2 model class directly to json-repair 0.61.2, but the experiment log only verifies the PARAMETER NAME 'schema' is present in `inspect.signature` output — not that the parameter's type contract accepts a Pydantic BaseModel class object. The json-repair library's `schema=` parameter in 0.61.x typically expects a JSON Schema dict (e.g. the output of `model_cls.model_json_schema()`), not a Pydantic model class directly. If the type contract is a dict, `schema=model_cls` would raise on every call, silently fall back to schema-less repair via the try/except, and make the schema-guided repair feature a permanent no-op with no runtime signal — the stated 'proven addition' would never actually run.
findings.location: What / Self-heal tweak 1 — schema-guided json-repair; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Change `schema=model_cls` to `schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()` if the parameter expects a JSON Schema dict, which is the common contract for schema-guided repair libraries. Update the AC test to assert the repaired output conforms to the schema, not merely that the kwarg was forwarded.

findings.finding: The plan states that a silent-success detection on the NativeOutput path routes through 'story mamba's shared LLMOutcome (resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT + sanitized diagnostic)', but classify_llm_failure in failure.py has no arm for UnretryableOutputError or StructuredOutputError — both fall through to the NEEDS_INVESTIGATION default (failure.py:256). The plan never specifies adding a new arm to _map() to produce the claimed CHANGE_INPUT routing, leaving the stated outcome unreachable as written.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section (refusal-as-content protocol paragraph); failure.py:229-256 (_map function); failure.py:232 (only content_filter handled via ctx.finish_reason, not via exception type)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section, either (a) add a step: 'at the NativeOutput catch site, attach `err.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=stop_reason))` (mirroring the PromptedOutput path which already surfaces finish_reason via check_stop_reason); also add an arm in failure.py._map() for UnretryableOutputError → CHANGE_INPUT' or (b) correct the plan to acknowledge that UnretryableOutputError propagates as an LLMError passthrough and the CHANGE_INPUT routing applies only to the downstream handler that already catches content_filter via ctx.finish_reason.

findings.finding: The plan claims a truncation-in-schema-valid verdict 'routes to INDETERMINATE' (AC#1) and that detected silent-successes go through 'story mamba's shared LLMOutcome (resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT + sanitized diagnostic),' but the routing protocol — which catch site translates the raised UnretryableOutputError into an INDETERMINATE verdict and into a CHANGE_INPUT LLMOutcome — is never specified. In the existing codebase, failure._map() only returns CHANGE_INPUT when ctx.finish_reason=='content_filter' and exc is None (line 232-233); for an UnretryableOutputError exception instance it falls to NEEDS_INVESTIGATION (line 256). The gate_dispatch degrade path (llm_unavailable=True → INDETERMINATE) is only triggered for LLMUnavailableError, not for UnretryableOutputError.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' / 'Verified library behavior'; AC#1; failure.py:229-256; gate_dispatch.py:444-452
findings.suggested_fix: In failure._map(), extend line 232 to: `if ctx.finish_reason in ('content_filter', 'length', 'max_tokens', 'refusal'): return ResolutionClass.CHANGE_INPUT` so classify_llm_failure(exc=None, ctx=ClassifyContext(finish_reason='length')) maps correctly. Separately, document in the plan which catch site (runner.py's generic handler, or a new explicit arm) converts UnretryableOutputError into LLMUnavailableError so the gate_dispatch degrade path fires.

findings.finding: The plan states that `check_stop_reason` raises `UnretryableOutputError` on truncation, yet AC1 says a truncated-but-schema-valid verdict 'routes to INDETERMINATE' — the glue between the raised exception and the INDETERMINATE routing is never specified. An executor cannot determine whether to wrap `check_stop_reason` in a try/catch on the NativeOutput branch and convert the exception, add a separate pre-check before calling `check_stop_reason`, or modify the existing helper. Without this, the implementation of the core correctness gap (runner.py:497-502) is ambiguous.
findings.location: What section — 'Truncation-within-a-schema-valid-verdict' + AC1 (`test_truncated_valid_verdict_routes_indeterminate`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'In the NativeOutput branch, after `check_stop_reason` raises `UnretryableOutputError`, the caller (runner.py) catches it and returns an `LLMOutcome(resolution_class=INDETERMINATE, diagnostic=...)` rather than propagating the exception — the same catch block that already handles this on the PromptedOutput path (or a shared helper if none exists yet).'

findings.finding: The plan body says `check_stop_reason` raises `UnretryableOutputError` on truncation/refusal (an exception path), yet AC#1 says a truncated-but-schema-valid verdict 'routes to INDETERMINATE' (a routing path). No conversion layer between the exception and the INDETERMINATE routing outcome is described anywhere in the plan. Separately, the Verified library behavior section says a detected silent-success is 'routed through story mamba's shared LLMOutcome (resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT)', while AC#1 says the same event 'routes to INDETERMINATE' — CHANGE_INPUT and INDETERMINATE are potentially different resolution_class values in story mamba's taxonomy, and the plan never equates them. An implementer following the body would raise an exception; an implementer following the AC would return a structured INDETERMINATE/CHANGE_INPUT verdict — two incompatible behaviors.
findings.location: 'What' section (truncation-conjunction paragraph) vs AC#1 vs Verified library behavior section (final paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add one sentence: 'The `UnretryableOutputError` raised by `check_stop_reason` is caught in the verdict-finalization path and converted to a story mamba `LLMOutcome(resolution_class=INDETERMINATE, ...)` (CHANGE_INPUT is the mamba label for this bucket — see story mamba §X); this conversion is already present for the PromptedOutput path and is extended to the NativeOutput path here.' Then update AC#1 to reference the same label consistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration-level AC (or explicitly document and justify its absence) that exercises the NativeOutput stop-reason check against a realistic provider boundary — e.g. a recorded-replay or provider-sandboxed test that sends a `length`/`content_filter` finish_reason through the real NativeOutput branch — so the fix is proven end-to-end and not only via mocked unit fixtures.
findings.finding: The NativeOutput stop-reason fix is verified exclusively by unit tests (`test_native_output_path_checks_stop_reason`, `tests/unit/test_silent_success.py`). The plan explicitly states that NativeOutput is the live OpenAI-strict-schema path in production. Per E6(f), when a fix closes a silent-success gap on a live production code path, at least one AC should exercise that path end-to-end (against a real model or a realistic provider-boundary integration test) — a unit test that mocks `finish_reason` cannot catch, for example, a provider returning the field under a different key or the check being wired to the wrong branch in the real execution flow.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > first bullet; What > NativeOutput stop-reason parity

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why the NativeOutput parity bug-fix cannot be landed independently before the two enhancements, or split it into a prerequisite ticket — each of the three changes has standalone value and independent tests, so the 'none is independently land-worthy' assertion needs explicit evidence of coupling (e.g., a shared test fixture or a code dependency that makes partial landing break CI).
findings.finding: The ticket bundles a correctness bug-fix (NativeOutput stop-reason parity) with two independent enhancements (schema-guided repair, faulty-output reask), each of which is independently valuable, testable, and releasable; the 'same seam' justification is a file-proximity argument, not a true coupling argument.
findings.location: Scope section; What section items 1–3; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Land the NativeOutput stop-reason parity fix (runner.py change + test_native_output_path_checks_stop_reason + test_zero_finding_clean_review_passes) as a standalone correctness ticket first. Group the two self-heal tweaks (schema-guided repair + faulty prior output in reask) in a follow-on ticket; they share the structured.py/runner.py reask path and have a weaker argument for cohesion with each other than with the parity fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the propagation path: either (a) widen gate_dispatch's catch to include `LLMRunnerError`/`StructuredOutputError`, (b) document that the workflow executor converts any step exception to `res.error`/`status='failed'` which then reaches `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`, or (c) show that `runner.run()` already converts `UnretryableOutputError` to `LLMUnavailableError` before it exits.
findings.finding: The plan claims `UnretryableOutputError` (raised by `check_stop_reason` on the NativeOutput path) routes to INDETERMINATE 'via `gate_dispatch`', but `gate_dispatch.py` only catches `LLMUnavailableError`. `UnretryableOutputError` is a subclass of `StructuredOutputError ⊂ LLMRunnerError ⊂ LLMError` — NOT a subclass of `LLMUnavailableError`. The propagation path from `_pai_structured` through the workflow executor (`run_workflow`) to the `gate_dispatch` INDETERMINATE degrade is never specified.
findings.location: What section — 'NativeOutput stop-reason parity'; AC #1: 'raises UnretryableOutputError → INDETERMINATE'; gate_dispatch.py catch blocks

findings.finding: `schema=model_cls` is proposed at `structured.py:161` inside `tolerant_parse(text: str)`, which has no `model_cls` parameter — adding schema-guided repair here requires adding a new optional parameter to `tolerant_parse` (and propagating it through `parse_structured`), expanding the shared API surface for a single call site (Rule-of-Three: `tolerant_parse` → `parse_structured` → `runner.py`; only one consumer ever has `model_cls` to pass).
findings.location: Output self-heal section; `src/rebar/llm/structured.py:161` / `tolerant_parse` / `parse_structured`
findings.suggested_fix: Move the schema-guided `repair_json` call into `parse_structured` rather than `tolerant_parse`, since `parse_structured(text, model_cls)` already has both arguments in scope — this avoids adding a new optional parameter to the shared `tolerant_parse` function and keeps the API surface unchanged.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding `check_stop_reason` to the NativeOutput branch routes a truncated/refused response to INDETERMINATE via the existing gate_dispatch path. However, `UnretryableOutputError` is a subclass of `StructuredOutputError → LLMRunnerError → LLMError` — it is NOT a subclass of `LLMUnavailableError`. `gate_dispatch.py` catches only `LLMUnavailableError` (lines 136, 173) for INDETERMINATE degradation. An `UnretryableOutputError` raised from the NativeOutput path would propagate uncaught through `produce_plan_review_verdict`, NOT producing INDETERMINATE — it would instead propagate to the outer caller as an unhandled `LLMError`.
findings.location: AC#1 — 'NativeOutput path raises UnretryableOutputError → INDETERMINATE'; What section — 'raises UnretryableOutputError → INDETERMINATE, matching the PromptedOutput path'

findings.finding: The plan contains a self-contradictory specification of the truncation-conjunction mechanism: the early 'What' section and AC #2 describe a 'routing' behavior ('a verdict that parses BUT whose finish_reason indicates truncation is treated as degraded (routed, not returned as PASS/BLOCK)'), while a later parenthetical in the same 'What' section explicitly retracts this ('There is no parses-but-truncated case to route separately: check_stop_reason runs BEFORE the parse, so truncation is caught as the exception, not as a schema-valid-but-stripped verdict'). An implementer reading AC #2 ('the truncation-conjunction routes degraded results') will attempt to build routing logic that the body says is unnecessary and non-existent.
findings.location: What section (truncation-conjunction paragraphs) and AC line 2 ('the truncation-conjunction routes degraded results')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the contradictory routing language with: 'When `check_stop_reason` detects a `length`/`max_tokens` finish_reason, it raises `UnretryableOutputError` before any schema parse — there is no post-parse routing step. The conjunction is: both `check_stop_reason` passing AND the schema parse succeeding are required for a valid result.' Then update AC #2 to read: 'the truncation signal raises `UnretryableOutputError` (not a routing decision), and a zero-finding clean review still passes untouched.'

findings.finding: AC #2's truncation-conjunction claim ('truncation-conjunction routes degraded results') has no named proving test. The only test cited in AC #2 is `test_zero_finding_clean_review_passes`, which verifies the clean/happy path — it does not exercise the truncation-conjunction path and cannot prove that a schema-valid-but-truncated response is handled correctly.
findings.location: AC #2, tests/unit/test_silent_success.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #2: 'and test `test_schema_valid_but_truncated_response_raises` exercises a canned finish_reason=length response whose body parses cleanly, asserting UnretryableOutputError is raised before the verdict is returned.'

findings.finding: The plan contains a direct internal contradiction about when truncation is caught: the early 'What' bullets say 'a verdict that parses BUT whose finish_reason indicates length/max_tokens truncation is treated as degraded (routed)' and AC #2 says 'the truncation-conjunction routes degraded results', but a later paragraph in the same 'What' section says 'There is no parses-but-truncated case to route separately: check_stop_reason runs BEFORE the parse, so truncation is caught as the exception, not as a schema-valid-but-stripped verdict.' An implementer reading the earlier text (and AC #2) would build a post-parse routing branch; an implementer reading the later text would build only a pre-parse exception. These are mechanically different implementations.
findings.location: What section (truncation-within-a-schema-valid-verdict bullets + refusal-as-content protocol paragraph) and AC #2
findings.suggested_fix: If the definitive behavior is pre-parse exception (check_stop_reason raises UnretryableOutputError before parse, so no post-parse routing exists), then: (a) remove or rewrite the early bullet about 'Schema-valid AND untruncated is the pass condition' / 'routed, not returned as PASS/BLOCK'; (b) update AC #2 to say 'a truncated NativeOutput response raises UnretryableOutputError → INDETERMINATE (same as PromptedOutput)' instead of 'routes degraded results'; (c) keep the clarifying paragraph as-is. If post-parse routing IS intended for some cases, describe the exact condition and code location, and reconcile with the 'check_stop_reason runs BEFORE the parse' statement.

findings.finding: The plan contains internally contradictory truncation-handling protocols: the 'What' section states 'There is no parses-but-truncated case to route separately: check_stop_reason runs BEFORE the parse, so truncation is caught as the exception' (negating the conjunction path), while AC line 2 states 'the truncation-conjunction routes degraded results' (asserting a conjunction routing path exists). An executor reading both is blocked: does the plan want a conjunction check that routes degraded-but-valid verdicts, or is truncation always an exception before parse?
findings.location: Plan 'What' section (refusal-as-content protocol paragraph, parenthetical 'There is no parses-but-truncated case'); AC line 2 ('the truncation-conjunction routes degraded results')
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that check_stop_reason always fires before parse (the cleaner design), update AC line 2 to: 'The verdict is validated by the existing parse_structured schema check; truncation is caught before parse via check_stop_reason and raises UnretryableOutputError — a zero-finding CLEAN review still passes untouched (test_zero_finding_clean_review_passes).' Remove the truncation-conjunction framing from the opening 'Why' bullet.
